comments.author: Abdalhadi Zatar
comments.comment:
The strongest part here is not “AI greenhouse”, it is the safety boundary around the AI.

Batching sensor reads, injecting action history, and enforcing irrigation cooldowns in the backend is exactly the right pattern: let the model reason, but let deterministic code protect the physical system.

One thing I’d test next is a failure-mode matrix: sensor drift, delayed readings, missed actuator commands, and bad model output.

comments.author: Thomas Geiger
comments.comment: Local agents get much more useful when you treat the model as the expensive part and the environment as the product: tight tool schemas, small reversible actions, cheap evals, and clear failure modes. The interesting metric is not tokens/sec, it is how often the agent can finish a task without escalating to a bigger model.

comments.author: NMG Labs
comments.comment: 800 to 60 with hidden-set execution is the right filter for agent builders. The part I’d love to see more hackathons copy is automatic cloning + F1-based eval, because it forces teams to treat reliability, reproducibility, and edge cases as first-class work, not polish after the demo.

comments.author: Anthony Munoz Cifuentes
comments.comment: The list quality point is dead on. In outbound, I’ve seen the biggest lift come from ranking accounts by trigger timing, not firmographics, because the same buyer ignores you in Q1 and replies in Q3 when budget, hiring, or pain changes. What signal do you trust most when deciding someone is worth contacting right now?

comments.author: Anirudh Madhavan
comments.comment: The unsexy part is where most agent projects win or die: retries, audit logs, permissions, fallback paths, and evals on real edge cases. A demo proves the happy path exists. Month six proves the workflow is narrow enough, measurable enough, and reliable enough to deserve automation.

comments.author: Jamal Reimer
comments.comment:
6-person teams only work when ownership is painfully explicit.

The hard part is not “no hierarchy,” it is keeping decision rights clear as the surface area grows - support, product, engineering, sales, and customers all start pulling at once.

The best teams I’ve worked with had high trust, but also very little ambiguity about who owns the next move.

comments.author: Nissim Ren Cohen
comments.comment:
More agents can look smarter simply because they get more chances to sample, retrieve, and self-correct.

The clean test is an ablation where solo, 3-agent, and 7-agent setups get the same context snapshot, tool budget, token ceiling, and retry policy. Then compare marginal quality gain per extra dollar and second, not just final judge score.

Curious if you saw any tasks where the 7-agent setup consistently beat solo after normalizing for token budget.

comments.author: Oliver W.
comments.comment: The strongest GenAI candidates I’ve seen can explain their eval loop as clearly as their model architecture. For this kind of role, I’d ask how they measure RAG quality beyond retrieval recall: answer faithfulness, tool failure recovery, latency budget, and cost per successful task.

comments.author: Rohith Thakurwar
comments.comment: The real gap is not benchmark score, it is recovery behavior after the first bad tool call. Open models are getting strong enough, but agent harnesses need better state tracking, rollback, permissioning, and evals against messy multi-step workflows before businesses will trust them. What eval would you use to prove an agent is reliable enough for daily ops?

comments.author: Tiger Analytics
comments.comment: Production AI usually fails less on model quality and more on handoffs: data freshness, permissions, monitoring, rollback paths, and ownership when the agent is wrong. The teams that win treat agents like distributed systems, not demos with a chat box.
